---
title: "JC Poultry Farm Dashboard"
format: dashboard
editor: visual
---

```{r warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}

# -----------------------------------------------------------
# Load libraries
# -----------------------------------------------------------

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(highcharter)
library(DT)
library(bslib)
library(bsicons)
library(shiny)
library(gt)
library(glue)
library(gtExtras)
library(kableExtra)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
library(formattable)
library(robotoolbox)
library(dm)
library(qcc)
library(plotly)
library(knitr)
library(gridExtra)
library(ggthemes)

# Source functions
source(file="./R-scripts/00-functions.R")

# -----------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Connect remotely to Kobotoolbox database via the API
#    
# -----------------------------------------------------------

## The first step is to get your API token from your Kobotoolbox account


mytoken <- "07cdf3134d0f4352b54cf83edaa59101bc02990e"


## With the token you can now setup the robotoolbox package.

kobo_setup(url = "https://kf.kobotoolbox.org/", token = mytoken)

# Now you can access all your projects and read the data from the project you picked. 
# You can list project using kobo_asset_list().

my_projects_list <- kobo_asset_list()

# Let’s pick the second project and load it

uid <- my_projects_list$uid[1]

# Initialize it as an asset
asset <- kobo_asset(uid)

# With the asset you can now read your data
df_raw <- kobo_data(asset)

# Archive raw data with today's date (flatten list columns from KoboToolbox)
archive_dir <- "data/archive"
if (!dir.exists(archive_dir)) dir.create(archive_dir, recursive = TRUE)
df_archive <- df_raw |> mutate(across(where(is.list), ~sapply(., paste, collapse = "; ")))
write.csv(df_archive, file.path(archive_dir, paste0("jc_farms_transaction_data_", format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d"), ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)

# -----------------------------------------------------------
# 2. (Optional) Clean or rename columns for easier reference
# -----------------------------------------------------------
# Sometimes R automatically converts special characters (spaces, parentheses).
# If your 'Amount (UGX)' column is not easy to reference, rename it:


df <- df_raw |> 
  rename(
    Amount_UGX = amount_manual,
    Batch_Number = batch
  ) |> 
  filter( account_type != "capex") |> 
  filter(Batch_Number != "3") |> 
  select(!("_attachments"))

transactions <- df

# write.csv(df, "data/jc_farms_transaction_data_20251228.csv",row.names = FALSE)

# -----------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Compute KPIs for all active batches
#    To add a new batch: add a row here + a markdown tab below
# -----------------------------------------------------------

batch_config <- tibble(
  batch_num = c("15", "16", "17", "18", "19"),
  status    = c("closed", "closed", "active", "active", "closed")
)

batch_config <- batch_config |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(num_birds = jc_get_bird_count(df, batch_num, status)) |>
  ungroup()

all_kpis <- setNames(
  lapply(seq_len(nrow(batch_config)), function(i) {
    jc_dashboard_kpis(df, batch_config$batch_num[i], batch_config$num_birds[i])
  }),
  paste0("kpis", batch_config$batch_num)
)

```

# Batch 15 (Closed)

```{r}
jc_render_batch_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis15"]])
```

## Per Bird

```{r}
jc_render_per_bird_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis15"]])
```

# Batch 16 (Closed)

```{r}
jc_render_batch_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis16"]])
```

## Per Bird

```{r}
jc_render_per_bird_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis16"]])
```

# Batch 17 (Active)

```{r}
jc_render_batch_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis17"]], status = "active")
```

## Per Bird

```{r}
jc_render_per_bird_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis17"]], status = "active")
```

# Batch 18 (Active)

```{r}
jc_render_batch_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis18"]], status = "active")
```

## Per Bird

```{r}
jc_render_per_bird_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis18"]], status = "active")
```

# Batch 19 (Closed)

```{r}
jc_render_batch_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis19"]], status = "closed")
```

## Per Bird

```{r}
jc_render_per_bird_kpis(all_kpis[["kpis19"]], status = "closed")
```

# Batch Trends and Patterns Analysis

Click link:

[JC Trends Report](trends_jc_farms.html){target="_blank"}

# Profit / Loss Statement

```{r}
# Read the Excel file

pnl_data <- read_excel("data/JC Poultry Farm - Business Operating Model_v1_250323.xlsx", 
                      sheet = "PnL",
                      skip = 3)  # Skip the first 3 rows to get to the data

# Clean and transform the data
pnl_statement <- pnl_data %>%
  # Remove any NA rows
  filter(!is.na(`Category`)) %>%
  # Rename columns for clarity
  rename(
    Category = `Category`,
    description = `Description`,
    "Batch 1" = `Amount (UGX)...3`,
    "Batch 2" = `Amount (UGX)...4`,
    "Batch 3" = `Amount (UGX)...5`
  ) %>%
  # Select only the relevant columns
  select(Category, "Batch 1", "Batch 2", "Batch 3")

# Get Batch 4-19 P&L dataframes and merge with original PnL
final_pnl <- pnl_statement
for (bn in as.character(4:19)) {
  batch_pnl <- jc_pnl_by_batch(df_raw, bn)
  final_pnl <- final_pnl |> left_join(batch_pnl, by = "Category")
}

# Format numbers with commas
final_pnl <- final_pnl %>%
  mutate(across(starts_with("Batch"), ~format(., big.mark = ",", scientific = FALSE)))

final_pnl |> 
  kbl(caption = "JC Farms Profit/Loss Statement") |>
  kable_paper("striped", full_width = F) %>%
  add_indent(c(2, 4, 5,6,7,9,10,11,12,13)) |> 
  row_spec(c(1,3,8,14), bold = T, color = "white", background = "#4287f5") 


  


```

# Data

```{r}

#| title: Batch Data Table 

batch_data <- df |>
  select(-c("start","end"))

datatable(batch_data, filter = "top")
```
